checks.a_analysis: The output content was thoroughly compared against the source PDF. All textual elements, including the report title, hospital survey information, infection statistics in tables, explanatory notes, and footer metadata, are directly present in the source. No fabricated sentences or paragraphs were identified.

The URL for the reference report (https://epi.dph.ncdhhs.gov/cd/hai/figures.html) and the generation dates (October 23, 2025) were verified against the source PDF. All information matches exactly; no contact information was fabricated.

The output contains all sections from both pages of the source PDF, including CAUTI, MRSA LabID, CDI LabID, CLABSI, and the unavailable data sections for SSI and VAE. No pages or whole sections are missing.

All tables in the output (Hospital Survey, Tables 1, 2, 3, and 4) contain the same number of data rows as presented in the source PDF. For instance, the survey table has 8 data rows, and the infection tables include specific unit types ('Adult/Ped Wards', 'All reporting units', or 'Facility-wide inpatient') exactly as shown in the original document.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: Each section was cross-referenced for completeness. Key explanatory notes, such as those regarding SIR calculations and baseline experience, were fully captured. The descriptions of data unavailability for specific procedures were also correctly transcribed.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The text in the output faithfully represents the source. Numbers (e.g., SIR values of 0.58, 0.61, 1.04, 0.75, 0.64, 0.56, 0.54, 0.76) and statistical categories ('Less than 1.0', 'No Conclusion') match perfectly. The alt text for the figures correctly incorporates the numerical values displayed on the charts in the source.

checks.a_analysis: The document content is processed across sections that correspond to the source document's logical divisions. Repeated header and footer information across PDF pages has been consolidated at the beginning and end of the JSON respectively, which is an expected behavior for accessibility and structure. No text blocks are duplicated in a way not present in the source.

The heading levels follow a logical progression from H2 for the main title to H3 for the hospital name. Section titles (e.g., '2024 Hospital Survey Information', 'Catheter-Associated Urinary Tract Infections (CAUTI)') are handled as structural section titles rather than explicit heading elements within the text blocks, which maintains a clean hierarchy. No levels are skipped (e.g., H2 to H4).

All tables (Tables 1-4 and the survey info table) maintain the correct row and column counts as seen in the source PDF. Table 1, for instance, correctly identifies 4 columns and the data rows for 'Adult/Ped Wards' and 'All reporting units'. The structural organization is consistent.

The JSON sections follow the sequential flow of the source document, moving through the header, the survey data, and each specific infection report section (CAUTI, MRSA, CDI, CLABSI, etc.) in the order they appear on the pages.

The source contains simple lists of notes or legend items. These are correctly captured as multi-line paragraphs, preserving the original formatting and meaning. No list nesting or numbering was corrupted.
The external URL 'https://epi.dph.ncdhhs.gov/cd/hai/figures.html' is correctly extracted and formatted as a link in the final section, matching the source PDF perfectly.

checks.a_analysis: The HTML output converts the multi-column and grid-based layout of the PDF into a single-column responsive layout. This is an expected transformation for accessibility and web standards. The logical flow of the document (Hospital Survey Information, then CAUTI, MRSA, CDI, etc.) is preserved correctly. The headers and sectioning match the source's intent.

All tables (Hospital Survey Information, Table 1, Table 2, Table 3, and Table 4) are correctly rendered as HTML tables. They include distinct headers, clear row/column separation, and readable content that matches the data in the source PDF.

Several meaningful images present in the source PDF are missing from the HTML rendering. Specifically, the map of North Carolina at the top of page 1 is replaced by placeholder alt-text in brackets. More critically, while Figure 1 (CAUTI) is rendered correctly, Figures 2 (MRSA LabID), 3 (CDI LabID), and 4 (CLABSI) are missing. For these figures, the text captions are present in the HTML, but the corresponding graphical charts are absent, appearing as empty white space where the data visualizations should be.
checks.b_evidence_source: The source PDF contains a map of North Carolina (page 1) and four data charts labeled Figures 1, 2, 3, and 4 across pages 1 and 2.
checks.b_evidence_output: The HTML screenshot shows placeholder text '[Map of North Carolina with a red star indicating the hospital's location.]' instead of the map image. It also shows text captions for Figure 2, Figure 3, and Figure 4, but no actual chart images are rendered above or near these captions.
checks.b_evidence_location: Figure captions for Figure 2, Figure 3, and Figure 4 in the HTML output.

checks.a_analysis: There is no evidence of duplicated body content. The repetitive header information from page 2 of the PDF is correctly unified into a single document structure in the HTML. Heading titles appearing in the Table of Contents and again in the body is standard accessible design and not considered a defect.
